title: Kohlhaas v. State
description: Whether the voter-approved Initiative 2, which established a nonpartisan open primary and ranked-choice voting, violates political parties' associational rights, the Alaska Constitution's provisions for electing the lieutenant governor or governor, or unconstitutionally burdens the right to vote.
plaintiff_argument: Kohlhaas argued that Initiative 2's nonpartisan primary and ranked-choice voting weakened political parties' associational rights, improperly paired governor/lieutenant governor candidates, and unconstitutionally burdened voters' rights.
defendant_argument: The State and Alaskans for Better Elections argued that Initiative 2 did not violate constitutional rights, decoupled state elections from party nominations, adhered to lieutenant governor election requirements, and imposed only minimal burdens on voting rights, while advancing important regulatory interests.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's judgment, upholding the constitutionality of Initiative 2's election reforms, including the nonpartisan open primary and ranked-choice voting.

title: State Department of Education & Early Development v. Alexander
description: Whether Alaska statutes allowing public funds for correspondence study allotments are facially unconstitutional because they permit the use of funds for private, including religious, educational institutions, or if they have a plainly legitimate sweep with many constitutional applications.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the statutes were facially unconstitutional or unconstitutional as applied to pay for private school tuition, violating the Alaska Constitution's prohibition against direct benefits to religious and private educational institutions, and that the Legislature intended an unconstitutional purpose.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that the statutes were not facially unconstitutional as they have many valid uses for allotment funds, and that any as-applied challenge to private school tuition funding could not proceed without joining the local school districts that approve such spending.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the superior court's judgment that the statutes were facially unconstitutional and remanded the case for further proceedings, declining to rule on whether using allotment funds for private school tuition is constitutional at this stage.

title: Valoaga v. State
description: Whether the Alaska Department of Corrections' use of a preponderance of evidence standard in prison disciplinary proceedings is constitutional, and whether its drug testing policy violates due process by not offering a saliva test as an alternative.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that DOC must prove violations by clear and convincing evidence, not preponderance of evidence, and that DOC's failure to offer a saliva test in lieu of a urine test violated his due process rights.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that the Alaska Constitution permits the use of a preponderance of the evidence standard in prison disciplinary proceedings and that the plaintiff's saliva test argument was not preserved for review.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's decision, ruling that the preponderance of evidence standard is constitutional in prison disciplinary proceedings and that the plaintiff's argument regarding saliva tests was waived.

title: Alaska Trappers Association v. City of Valdez
description: Whether the City of Valdez's ordinance limiting animal trapping in certain areas is invalid due to state law preemption, or if it is a valid exercise of municipal authority to regulate land use and public safety.
plaintiff_argument: The Alaska Trappers Association argued that the Valdez ordinance is preempted by state law, infringes on state-vested authority over fish and game management, and violates the Alaska Constitution's article VIII by not applying the sustained yield principle.
defendant_argument: The City of Valdez argued that, as a home rule municipality, it has constitutional and statutory authority to regulate land use and public safety, and its ordinance is not expressly or impliedly prohibited by state law, as its impact on natural resource management is incidental.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's order granting summary judgment to the City of Valdez, ruling that the ordinance is a valid exercise of municipal authority and not preempted by state law.

title: State v. McKelvey
description: Whether police must obtain a warrant before using an airplane with a zoom lens to photograph a person's private yard, specifically their home's curtilage.
plaintiff_argument: The State argued that no warrant was needed because small airplane travel is common in Alaska, any passenger could observe a yard, and the 'open view' doctrine should apply to aerial observations, especially with commercially available technology.
defendant_argument: McKelvey argued that the warrantless aerial observation of his home with a telephoto lens constituted an illegal search under the Alaska Constitution.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the court of appeals' decision, ruling that the Alaska Constitution requires law enforcement officials to obtain a warrant before using aircraft and vision-enhancing technology to observe a home's curtilage that is protected from ground-level observation.

title: Vazquez v. State
description: Whether Representative Jennifer Armstrong met the Alaska Constitution's three-year state residency requirement to be eligible to serve in the legislature.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, Liz Vazquez, argued that Representative Armstrong did not establish Alaska residency until at least June 7, 2019, which would make her ineligible for office by the June 1, 2022 filing deadline.
defendant_argument: The defendant, Jennie Armstrong, maintained that she became an Alaska resident on May 20, 2019, making her eligible to serve in the legislature.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's decision that Representative Armstrong met the constitutional and statutory residency requirements and is eligible to serve in the legislature.

title: Native Village of Kwinhagak v. State, Department of Health & Social Services
description: Whether the Office of Children's Services (OCS) must follow civil commitment statutes or provide timely notice and hearing when admitting a child in its custody to a psychiatric hospital, and whether Mira's prolonged hospitalization without a prompt hearing violated her constitutional due process rights.
plaintiff_argument: The Tribe argued that OCS was required to follow the civil commitment statutes with their strict timelines and procedural safeguards when hospitalizing a child for psychiatric care, and that Mira's prolonged hospitalization without a prompt hearing violated her constitutional due process rights.
defendant_argument: OCS argued that its authority under CINA statutes allowed it to admit children in its custody for psychiatric care without following civil commitment statutes, and that the existing Hooper Bay injunction, which required a hearing within 30 days, satisfied due process.
decision_outcome: The court rejected the Tribe's argument that OCS must follow civil commitment statutes for Mira's hospitalization but found that Mira's constitutional right to procedural due process was violated by the lack of timely notice and hearing, reversing the superior court's order.

title: Sitka Tribe of Alaska v. State
description: Whether the Alaska Constitution requires the Department of Fish and Game to provide all relevant information to the Board of Fisheries for resource management, whether the Sitka Tribe of Alaska faced irreparable harm warranting a preliminary injunction, and whether the Tribe was the prevailing party for purposes of awarding attorney's fees.
plaintiff_argument: The Alaska Constitution's sustained yield clause mandates the Department to provide all relevant information to the Board, the Tribe faced irreparable harm needing a preliminary injunction, and the Tribe was the prevailing party for attorney's fees due to its statutory victory.
defendant_argument: There is no constitutional duty to provide all relevant information beyond existing doctrines like 'hard look,' the Tribe did not demonstrate irreparable harm, and the Tribe was not the sole prevailing party for attorney's fees due to the constitutional claim loss.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's decisions, ruling that there is no constitutional duty for the Department to provide all relevant information to the Board, that the Tribe did not demonstrate irreparable harm for a preliminary injunction, and that neither party was the sole prevailing party for attorney's fees.

title: Taylor v. Alaska Legislative Affairs Agency
description: Whether the Alaska Constitution bars a lawsuit brought by the Attorney General against the Legislative Affairs Agency to resolve a dispute between executive and legislative branches over an appropriations bill's effective date, where the plaintiff argues for common law authority and challenging non-legislative acts, and the defendant argues such a suit is barred as an action brought in the name of the State against the legislature.
plaintiff_argument: Attorney General Taylor argued that he has common law authority to sue on the State's behalf, distinct from the governor's constitutional authority, and that his suit targeted non-legislative administrative acts of the Legislative Affairs Agency, not legislative acts, thus not violating Article III, Section 16.
defendant_argument: The Legislative Affairs Agency contended that Article III, Section 16 of the Alaska Constitution explicitly bars the governor from suing the legislature, and in substance, the Attorney General's suit, brought in the name of the State, was an action against the legislature, aiming to influence legislative policy, which is prohibited.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's decision, ruling that Attorney General Taylor's lawsuit against the Legislative Affairs Agency was unconstitutional under Article III, Section 16 of the Alaska Constitution, as it was an action brought in the name of the State against the legislature.

title: Williams Alaska Petroleum, Inc. v. State
description: Whether Williams Alaska Petroleum, Inc. is strictly liable for environmental contamination by sulfolane and PFAS and the associated costs and damages, with the State and Flint Hills arguing for Williams's liability and Williams denying liability while asserting errors in the lower court's findings and constitutional violations.
plaintiff_argument: The State and Flint Hills argue that Williams is strictly liable for hazardous substance releases (sulfolane and PFAS) and responsible for associated damages, response costs, and remediation efforts.
defendant_argument: Williams argues that sulfolane was not properly defined as a hazardous substance, the awarded damages and response costs were erroneous, their due process rights were violated, and the decision constituted an unconstitutional taking.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed most of the superior court's findings, holding Williams strictly liable for sulfolane and PFAS contamination, awarding damages and response costs to the State and Flint Hills, and upholding contribution awards, but remanded the injunctive relief for more specificity.

title: In re Abigail B.
description: Whether the State's prolonged pre-evaluation detentions of individuals awaiting mental health evaluations violated their substantive due process rights.
plaintiff_argument: Abigail B. and Jethro S. argued that their lengthy pre-evaluation detentions violated their substantive due process rights because the detentions' nature and duration were not reasonably related to the sole purpose of immediate transportation for evaluation.
defendant_argument: The State argued for broader purposes of pre-evaluation detention, including protecting individuals and society from harm, and contended that the detentions were reasonably related to these broader purposes.
decision_outcome: The court vacated the superior court orders that had authorized the State's continued pre-evaluation detention of Abigail B. and Jethro S. following their review hearings.

title: In re 2021 Redistricting Cases
description: Whether the Alaska Redistricting Board's 2021 legislative redistricting plans (both initial and amended) complied with the Alaska Constitution's requirements for district formation, specifically regarding gerrymandering, socioeconomic integration, compactness, and public hearing procedures.
plaintiff_argument: The challengers argued that the Board's plans constituted unconstitutional political gerrymandering, violated due process and public hearing requirements, failed to adequately consider socioeconomic integration and compactness for certain House districts (e.g., Cantwell Appendage, Valdez/Mat-Su pairing), and resulted in overpopulated districts.
defendant_argument: The Alaska Redistricting Board contended that its plans met constitutional requirements, followed the Hickel process for redistricting, and did not engage in unconstitutional gerrymandering. They argued that the superior court erred in its 'hard look' analysis, its interpretation of public hearing requirements, and its findings regarding socioeconomic integration and compactness for the challenged districts.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's finding that the Senate districts (K, E, L) constituted unconstitutional political gerrymandering, reversed the 'hard look' invalidation of House Districts 3 and 4, reversed the finding that House District 36 was constitutional due to the 'Cantwell Appendage,' affirmed House Districts 29 and 30, and affirmed the order to implement Option 2 as an interim plan while remanding for the Board to justify it as a final plan.

title: Knolmayer v. McCollum
description: Whether Alaska Statute 09.55.548(b), which reduces medical malpractice awards by amounts compensated by collateral sources, violates the Equal Protection Guarantee of the Alaska Constitution when applied to a plaintiff whose insurer has a contractual right to reimbursement.
plaintiff_argument: McCollum argues that AS 09.55.548(b) should not apply to her ERISA-governed health plan because it is a "federal program," ERISA preempts the state law, an assignment of the subrogation claim allows her to recover, and ultimately, the statute as applied violates equal protection by creating a double deduction.
defendant_argument: Knolmayer argues that AS 09.55.548(b) applies to McCollum's ERISA plan, which is not a "federal program," and ERISA does not preempt it. He also contends that the statute's classifications are constitutional and serve the legitimate purpose of preventing double recoveries without abrogating insurers' subrogation rights.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court ruled that Alaska Statute 09.55.548(b) violates the state's equal protection clause when applied to medical malpractice claimants whose insurers have contractual reimbursement rights, vacating the lower court's order.

title: Short v. State
description: Whether the Higher Education Investment Fund (HEIF) is subject to the constitutional budgetary "sweep" provision, requiring its unappropriated funds to be deposited into the Constitutional Budget Reserve (CBR).
plaintiff_argument: The HEIF is not sweepable because its funds have already been validly appropriated and invested, and prior legal precedent either does not apply to this specific provision or should be overruled.
defendant_argument: The HEIF is sweepable because it resides in the general fund and, as per established case law, requires further legislative appropriation before expenditures, thus making its funds "available for appropriation."
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's decision that the Higher Education Investment Fund is sweepable under the relevant constitutional provision.

title: Department of Corrections v. Stefano
description: Whether the superior court has appellate jurisdiction to review the Department of Corrections' decision to remove an inmate from electronic monitoring and return them to prison, and whether such a decision implicates the constitutional guarantee of due process.
plaintiff_argument: Stefano argued that his removal from electronic monitoring implicated his constitutional right to rehabilitation and a protected liberty interest, thus the superior court had appellate jurisdiction to review the DOC's decision.
defendant_argument: The Department of Corrections argued that the superior court lacked appellate jurisdiction, that removal from electronic monitoring does not implicate a constitutional right to rehabilitation, and that its decision was not an adjudicative proceeding producing a record capable of appellate review.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that due process applies to removal from electronic monitoring, recognizing a prisoner's limited liberty interest, but concluded the superior court lacked appellate jurisdiction because the DOC's termination decision was not an adjudicative proceeding with a reviewable record. The court vacated the superior court's decision and remanded, instructing Stefano to convert his appeal into a civil action.

title: State v. The Alaska Legislative Council
description: Whether forward-funded appropriations for public education are consistent with the annual budgeting process established by the Alaska Constitution, with the Legislative Council arguing they are permissible and the Governor arguing they violate the annual appropriations model.
plaintiff_argument: The Legislative Council argued that the forward-funded appropriations were consistent with the legislature's constitutional authority to fund public education, did not violate any specific constitutional provision, and that the governor's refusal to disburse funds violated his duty to faithfully execute the laws, as the Constitution places no temporal limits on the legislature's appropriation power.
defendant_argument: The Governor argued that forward funding for public education was unconstitutional because it violated the annual appropriations model established by the Alaska Constitution, the prohibition on dedicating revenues, and the governor's right to veto or reduce appropriations.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the superior court's decision, ruling that the forward-funded appropriations are unconstitutional because they are inconsistent with the annual budgeting process implied by the Alaska Constitution.

title: State v. Graham
description: Whether the superior court erred in applying sentencing standards and admitting victim impact evidence in the sentencing of Stacey Graham for vehicular homicide, and whether the sentencing judge should be recused.
plaintiff_argument: The State of Alaska argued that the superior court properly applied sentencing benchmarks and aggravating factors, and correctly considered general deterrence and community condemnation. While conceding evidentiary errors regarding police testimony and tribute videos, they contended these errors did not necessitate the recusal of the sentencing judge.
defendant_argument: Stacey Graham argued that the superior court imposed an excessive sentence by misapplying sentencing benchmarks and an aggravating factor, improperly relying on general deterrence and community condemnation, and admitting prejudicial victim impact evidence, thus requiring resentencing by a different judge.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the decision of the court of appeals on sentencing standards, vacated Graham's sentence due to evidentiary errors, and remanded the case for resentencing by the same judge, with instructions for reviewing victim tribute videos.

title: Association of Village Council Presidents Regional Housing Authority v. Mael
description: Whether the housing authority had a tort duty to inspect a residential boiler and negligently caused an explosion, and whether the statutory damages cap on noneconomic damages applies separately to distinct injuries of multiple family members or is aggregated.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the housing authority had an independent tort duty to inspect the boiler, which it negligently violated, causing the explosion. The plaintiff also argued that Dietrich's noneconomic damages cap violated due process, and that family members' emotional distress damages should not be aggregated with Dietrich's damages under a single cap.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that no tort duty existed or, if it did, it had expired, and that the boiler explosion was caused by a product defect. The defendant also argued that all noneconomic damages, including those for family members' emotional distress, should be aggregated under a single statutory damages cap.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's judgment, concluding that the housing authority owed a tort duty to inspect the boiler, that the jury had sufficient evidence of negligence, that the damages cap was properly applied to Dietrich's award, and that family members' emotional distress damages were properly excluded from that cap.

title: Dutchess v. Dutch
description: Whether the father should have sole legal custody regarding vaccination decisions for the children, with the father advocating for vaccination based on medical recommendations and the mother objecting on religious grounds.
plaintiff_argument: The mother objects to vaccinating the children on religious grounds and argues that the court's decision infringes on her free exercise of religion and violates due process rights.
defendant_argument: The father wants to vaccinate the children per their pediatrician's recommendation, believing it is in their best interests, and was granted sole legal custody for vaccination decisions.
decision_outcome: The superior court's order granting the father sole legal custody with regard to vaccination decisions was affirmed, finding that the decision served the children's best interests.

title: Sagoonick v. State
description: Whether the State of Alaska's resource development policies, which contribute to climate change, violate the constitutional rights of young Alaskans to a stable climate system and protection of natural resources, or if these are non-justiciable political questions for the legislative and executive branches.
plaintiff_argument: Young Alaskans argued that the State's resource development policies and actions contribute to climate change, adversely affecting their lives and violating their constitutional rights, including a fundamental right to a stable climate system and the State's public trust duty to protect natural resources.
defendant_argument: The State argued that the plaintiffs' claims involve non-justiciable political questions, better left to the legislative and executive branches, and that the Department's denial of the rulemaking petition was not arbitrary and complied with statutory requirements.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's dismissal of the plaintiffs' lawsuit, concluding that the injunctive relief claims presented non-justiciable political questions and that declaratory relief would not meaningfully settle legal relations.

title: Department of Administration v. Retired Public Employees of Alaska, Inc.
description: Whether the State of Alaska's 2014 redesign of the dental insurance plan for public retirees unconstitutionally diminished accrued benefits, and whether the premiums paid by retirees should be considered when assessing the plan's value.
plaintiff_argument: The 2014 dental plan diminished retirees' accrued benefits, and the cost of premiums should be irrelevant to the constitutional diminishment analysis, which should focus on coverage.
defendant_argument: The dental coverage was not an accrued benefit, or if it was, the superior court erred by refusing to consider premium rates in its diminishment analysis.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court vacated the superior court's judgment, agreeing that the option to purchase dental insurance is an accrued benefit but remanding the case for reevaluation because the superior court erred in ignoring premium reductions when assessing whether the benefit was diminished.

title: Dunleavy v. Alaska Legislative Council ex rel. Alaska State Legislature
description: Whether legislative inaction on gubernatorial appointments during a public health emergency is tantamount to a denial of confirmation, precluding appointees from serving as recess appointments, or if appointees remain in office until an affirmative legislative vote.
plaintiff_argument: The Legislative Council argued that legislative inaction on gubernatorial appointments, as defined by statute, constitutes a denial of confirmation, making those appointees ineligible to continue serving as recess appointments.
defendant_argument: The Governor argued that his appointees remain in office and continue to serve until the legislature affirmatively votes on their confirmation, contending that laws defining inaction as rejection violate the Alaska Constitution.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court reversed the superior court's judgment, concluding that state laws defining legislative inaction as a denial of confirmation for gubernatorial appointees violate the Alaska Constitution.

title: State v. Arctic Village Council
description: Whether the absentee ballot witness requirement unconstitutionally burdened the right to vote for Alaskans during the COVID-19 pandemic.
plaintiff_argument: The absentee ballot witness requirement unconstitutionally burdened the fundamental right to vote, especially for vulnerable individuals and those adhering to strict social distancing during the COVID-19 pandemic, forcing them to choose between their health and exercising their right to vote.
defendant_argument: The witness requirement was a legitimate measure to deter voter fraud and promote voter confidence, and the plaintiffs' lawsuit was barred by laches due to their unreasonable delay in filing the suit.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's grant of a preliminary injunction, finding that the absentee ballot witness requirement impermissibly burdened the right to vote in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic.

title: State v. Recall Dunleavy
description: Whether the recall committee's application to recall Governor Dunleavy satisfied the legal and factual sufficiency requirements for presentation to the voters, with the recall committee arguing it was sufficient and the State arguing it was not.
plaintiff_argument: The recall committee argued that its application, which cited lack of fitness, incompetence, and neglect of duties as grounds for recall with specific allegations, was legally and factually sufficient.
defendant_argument: The State of Alaska, through the Division of Elections, argued that the recall application was not legally or factually sufficient to be certified.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court affirmed the superior court's decision, ruling that the recall committee's application (with one minor exception) was legally and factually sufficient, allowing the recall process to proceed to the signature-gathering phase.

title: State v. Galvin
description: Whether the Division of Elections' 2020 general election ballot design, which omitted a candidate's nonpartisan voter registration, violated state statutory directives and the candidate's right to free political association, thereby justifying a preliminary injunction.
plaintiff_argument: Alyse Galvin argued the ballot design violated a state statute requiring party affiliation to be designated on the ballot and infringed upon her constitutional right to free political association by omitting her nonpartisan voter registration.
defendant_argument: The Division of Elections contended that 'party affiliation' and 'party designation' were synonymous or that nonpartisan status isn't 'party affiliation,' and that granting a preliminary injunction would imperil the public interest in a timely and orderly election.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the superior court's denial of Alyse Galvin's request for a preliminary injunction, concluding that granting the injunction would have threatened the public interest in a successful and timely election, regardless of the merits of her claims.

title: Watson v. State
description: Whether the Alaska statute that excludes misdemeanor traffic violations from juvenile court jurisdiction for minors, requiring them to be charged as adults, while allowing felony traffic offenses to remain in juvenile court, violates a minor's right to equal protection under the Alaska Constitution.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, Elizabeth Watson, argued that the statute violates her right to equal protection because it unfairly treats first-time misdemeanor DUI offenders differently than other juvenile misdemeanors and felony DUI offenses, denying them the rehabilitative benefits of the juvenile court system.
defendant_argument: The defendant, the State of Alaska, argued that the statute is constitutional because treating misdemeanor traffic violations consistently promotes public safety, and the exception for felony traffic offenses reasonably protects juveniles from the harsh collateral consequences of a felony conviction.
decision_outcome: The court concluded that the statute is constitutional and affirmed the judgment of the district court, ruling that the classifications are closely related to important state interests.

title: Metcalfe v. State
description: Whether the repeal of a statutory right allowing former state employees to reinstate their retirement benefits and credited service diminished or impaired an "accrued benefit" protected by the Alaska Constitution, Article XII, section 7.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the statutory right to reinstate previous retirement benefits was an "accrued benefit" protected by the Alaska Constitution, and its repeal was unconstitutional.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that the reinstatement right was not an "accrued benefit" and that former members, like the plaintiff, were not protected by the constitutional provision.
decision_outcome: The Alaska Supreme Court reversed the superior court's summary judgment, ruling that the statutory reinstatement right was an accrued benefit protected by the constitution, and remanded the case for further proceedings.

title: Muth v. Voe; Doe v. Abbott; Muth v. PFLAG
description: Whether temporary injunctions preventing the Texas Department of Family and Protective Services from classifying gender-transition therapy for children as child abuse should be dissolved.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs (families, PFLAG, and Dr. Mooney) argued that the Texas Department of Family and Protective Services' classification of gender-transition therapy for children as child abuse violates the law, and thus the injunctions against this classification should remain.
defendant_argument: The defendants (Texas Department of Family and Protective Services and the State of Texas) argued that the classification of gender-transition therapy for children as child abuse is lawful, and that the injunctions against this policy should be dissolved, especially since such treatments have been outlawed in Texas.
decision_outcome: The court ruled with the Texas Department of Family and Protective Services, dissolving the temporary injunctions that prevented the department from classifying gender-transition therapy for children as child abuse.

title: PFLAG v. Office of the Attorney General
description: Whether the Attorney General's civil investigative demand for documents from PFLAG, related to an affidavit concerning "alternative avenues to maintain [transgender] care in Texas" post-SB 14, is valid and enforceable under the Deceptive Trade Practices Act.
plaintiff_argument: The Attorney General argued that PFLAG likely possessed information relevant to an investigation into medical providers who may be deceptively billing for transgender treatments outlawed by SB 14, and that the CID was based on a reasonable belief and subject to privacy protections.
defendant_argument: PFLAG argued that the CID was overbroad, sought irrelevant and privileged information, and infringed on privacy and constitutional rights, asserting its documents would not corroborate DTPA violations.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the district court's judgment and injunction, ruling that PFLAG must produce responsive documents to the Attorney General's revised civil investigative demand, subject to recognized privileges and redactions for member anonymity.

title: Paxton v. American Oversight
description: Whether the Public Information Act authorizes district courts to issue writs of mandamus against constitutional executive officers like the Governor and Attorney General.
plaintiff_argument: American Oversight argued that section 552.321(b) of the Public Information Act empowers district courts to issue writs of mandamus compelling executive officers to provide public information.
defendant_argument: The Governor and Attorney General (the State) argued that district courts lack jurisdiction to issue writs of mandamus against constitutional executive officers, contending that section 552.321(b) is merely a venue provision and does not override the Supreme Court's exclusive jurisdiction under section 22.002(c).
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the judgment of the court of appeals and directed the district court to dismiss the mandamus petition for lack of jurisdiction.

title: Texas Department of Family & Protective Services v. Grassroots LeaderShip, Inc.
description: Whether Texas courts are constitutionally authorized to adjudicate moot cases that raise questions of considerable public importance, with petitioners arguing that they are not and respondents arguing that various exceptions to mootness should allow for adjudication.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that the case was not moot due to potential re-detention or that existing mootness exceptions like 'capable-of-repetition-yet-evading-review' applied, or that the 'public-interest exception' should be adopted to allow a ruling on the merits of the rule's invalidity.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that the claims were moot because the detainees had been released, no existing mootness exception applied, and there is no 'public-interest exception' to mootness under the Texas Constitution.
decision_outcome: The Texas Supreme Court reversed the court of appeals' judgment on jurisdiction, vacated its judgment on the merits, and dismissed the case for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction, holding that Texas courts are not constitutionally authorized to adjudicate moot cases under a public-interest exception.

title: Ex Parte David Leonard Wood
description: Whether the Texas Constitution requires a less stringent standard than "clear and convincing" for actual innocence claims in capital habeas corpus cases, and if the court's remand order should include specific directives for DNA testing.
plaintiff_argument: The Applicant (plaintiff) argues that the court should apply a less stringent standard for actual innocence claims in capital cases under the Texas Constitution and that the remand order should explicitly direct DNA testing given new evidence.
defendant_argument: The court's (defendant's) remand order for further evidence development, without specific directives for DNA testing or addressing the standard of proof for actual innocence, reflects an adherence to existing procedures and the "clear and convincing" standard.
decision_outcome: The Court issued a remand order to the lower court for further development of the evidence the Applicant presents, without providing specific guidance on the scope of development or directing DNA testing.

title: Southern Methodist University v. South Central Jurisdictional Conference of the United Methodist Church
description: Whether SMU could lawfully amend its articles of incorporation to remove the South Central Jurisdictional Conference's authority without the Conference's affirmative consent.
plaintiff_argument: The South Central Jurisdictional Conference argued that SMU's articles of incorporation explicitly require their consent for any amendments, and SMU's unilateral attempt to eliminate their authority was unlawful.
defendant_argument: Southern Methodist University argued that the case involves neutral principles of law for corporate governance, and that a general statutory limitation on lawsuits (Business Organizations Code § 20.002) forecloses the Conference's ability to challenge the amendments.
decision_outcome: The court decided that the South Central Jurisdictional Conference is allowed to seek judicial interpretation regarding SMU's articles of incorporation and pursue its contract claim, thereby upholding the Conference's right to control SMU based on its articles of incorporation.

title: Paxton v. Annunciation House
description: Whether the Attorney General has the constitutional and statutory authority to file a quo warranto action against Annunciation House for alleged alien harboring, and whether the records-request statute is facially unconstitutional or infringes on religious liberty or privacy rights, with the plaintiff (Annunciation House) arguing against such authority and constitutionality, and the defendant (Texas Attorney General) asserting it.
plaintiff_argument: Annunciation House argued that the Attorney General's proposed quo warranto action was impermissible under RFRA, preempted by federal law, and based on unconstitutionally vague statutes, and that the records-request statute was facially unconstitutional under the First and Fourth Amendments.
defendant_argument: The Attorney General argued he possessed constitutional authority to file quo warranto actions for corporate criminal acts, that RFRA arguments were premature, state statutes were not preempted or vague, and that the records-request statute was constitutional.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas reversed the trial court's judgment, vacated the injunction against records requests, and remanded the case for further proceedings, effectively allowing the Attorney General to proceed with his actions.

title: Stary v. Ethridge
description: Whether constitutional due process requires clear and convincing evidence to support a protective order prohibiting all contact between a parent and her children for a period exceeding two years, where the plaintiff argues it is tantamount to parental rights termination and the defendant argues it is not.
plaintiff_argument: The protective order prohibiting all contact with her children for her lifetime without clear and convincing evidence violated her constitutional right to due process, as it is tantamount to termination of her parental rights.
defendant_argument: Protective orders of lengthy duration are not equivalent to termination of parental rights because parents retain ancillary rights and have opportunities for review, thus not requiring a heightened evidentiary burden.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas reversed the court of appeals' judgment and remanded the case, holding that due process requires clear and convincing evidence to support a protective order prohibiting parent-child contact for over two years and that the child's best interest must be considered.

title: Commons of Lake Houston v. City of Houston
description: Whether a city's amended floodplain ordinance, adopted as a valid exercise of police power and to comply with a federal flood-insurance program, can cause a compensable regulatory taking of private property, with the plaintiff arguing it can and the defendant arguing it cannot.
plaintiff_argument: The City's amended ordinance, which significantly increased elevation requirements in a floodplain, caused a regulatory taking of its property under the Texas Constitution by rendering many lots undevelopable and destroying expected profits.
defendant_argument: The City's amended ordinance was a valid exercise of its police power to promote public health and safety and to comply with the federal National Flood Insurance Program, and therefore, it cannot constitute a compensable taking. The plaintiff's claim is also unripe and they lack standing.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas reversed the court of appeals' judgment, holding that a regulatory taking can occur even if an ordinance is a valid exercise of police power or complies with a federal program, and that the plaintiff's claim is ripe and they have standing. The case was remanded to the trial court for further proceedings.

title: Perez v. City of San Antonio
description: Whether Article I, Section 6-a of the Texas Constitution imposes a categorical bar on any limitation of any religious service, regardless of the sort of limitation and the government's interest in that limitation.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs (Appellants) argued that Section 6-a of the Texas Constitution creates a categorical bar on any limitation of religious services, even prohibiting the City from attempting to satisfy strict scrutiny.
defendant_argument: The defendant (City of San Antonio) implicitly argued that Section 6-a does not impose such a categorical bar and that the City can impose limitations on religious services, particularly those related to the preservation and maintenance of public lands.
decision_outcome: The court decided that Article I, Section 6-a of the Texas Constitution does not impose a categorical bar on limitations of religious services that result from the government's preservation and maintenance of public lands, effectively rejecting the plaintiffs' broad interpretation.

title: Elliott v. City of College Station
description: Whether local regulation in an extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) without a corresponding right to vote in local elections is incompatible with the Texas Constitution's "republican form of government" requirement, with plaintiffs asserting it is unconstitutional and defendants arguing it is a political question not for the courts.
plaintiff_argument: Local regulation of ETJ residents without voting rights is facially unconstitutional under the Texas Constitution's "republican form of government" limitation, and the City's ordinances should be declared void and unenforceable.
defendant_argument: The constitutional claim is nonjusticiable because the form of local government is a political question, and the plaintiffs lack standing and their claims are not ripe for adjudication.
decision_outcome: The court vacated the lower court judgments and remanded the case to the trial court with instructions to abate proceedings, allowing the property owners a reasonable opportunity to complete the statutory opt-out process before judicial review of constitutional claims.

title: Ex parte Charette
description: Whether the Texas Ethics Commission (TEC) has exclusive jurisdiction over alleged campaign-law violations, making prior TEC proceedings a jurisdictional prerequisite for criminal prosecution, with the plaintiff arguing that the TEC does, and the defendant arguing that district attorneys retain independent prosecutorial power without such a prerequisite.
plaintiff_argument: The Texas Ethics Commission (TEC) has exclusive jurisdiction over campaign-law violations, and a prosecutor lacks authority to pursue criminal charges without prior TEC proceedings and referral, which constitutes a violation of due process and a lack of trial court subject matter jurisdiction.
defendant_argument: The district court has original jurisdiction over misdemeanors, and district attorneys have constitutional authority to investigate and prosecute crimes independently; TEC proceedings are civil and not a jurisdictional prerequisite for criminal prosecution.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the court of appeals' decision, ruling that the Texas Ethics Commission (TEC) has exclusive jurisdiction to make an initial determination regarding campaign-law violations, and therefore, the trial court lacked subject matter jurisdiction without prior TEC proceedings, ordering the dismissal of the indictments.

title: In re Dallas County
description: Whether Senate Bill 1045, which creates the Fifteenth Court of Appeals with statewide jurisdiction and specifies the appointment process for its justices, is constitutional.
plaintiff_argument: Dallas County argued that Senate Bill 1045 is unconstitutional because the Fifteenth Court's statewide geographic range, its subject-matter-based jurisdictional scope, and the appointment process for its initial justices violate the Texas Constitution, therefore the Third Court should retain jurisdiction.
defendant_argument: HHSC argued that Dallas County lacked standing, the issue was not ripe, and that S.B. 1045 is constitutional, as the legislature has broad authority to create courts and define their jurisdiction, and the judge appointment process is valid.
decision_outcome: The court denied Dallas County's requested relief, holding that Senate Bill 1045 is constitutional, meaning the appeal must be transferred to the Fifteenth Court of Appeals as scheduled.

title: Borgelt v. Austin Firefighters Association
description: Whether Article 10 of the collective-bargaining agreement between the City of Austin and its firefighters, which grants "Association Business Leave" (paid time off for union activities), constitutes an unconstitutional "gift" of public resources to a private party under the Texas Constitution's Gift Clauses, with petitioners arguing it impermissibly benefits the union through misused leave for improper private and political purposes, and defendants arguing it is a negotiated benefit with legitimate public purposes and sufficient controls.
plaintiff_argument: The petitioners argued that Article 10's "Association Business Leave" provision is an unconstitutional gift of public funds, alleging that the leave is routinely misused for private purposes, including political activities and fundraising, thereby impermissibly benefiting the Austin Firefighters Association rather than serving a legitimate public purpose.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that Article 10, as written and properly interpreted, imposes limits on the use of "Association Business Leave" that prevent improper uses, ensuring it serves a legitimate public purpose as a negotiated benefit within the collective-bargaining agreement and thus does not violate the Gift Clauses.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed that Article 10 of the collective-bargaining agreement does not violate the Gift Clauses of the Texas Constitution when properly construed, but reversed the award of fees and sanctions against the petitioners under the Texas Citizens Participation Act.

title: In re State
description: Whether Harris County's Uplift Harris program, which provides direct cash payments to individuals without specific public control or monitoring of expenditures, violates the Texas Constitution's prohibitions against gratuitous grants of public money for private purposes.
plaintiff_argument: The State (Relator) argues that the Uplift Harris program likely violates the Texas Constitution by improperly granting public money to individuals without retaining public control, constituting irreparable harm that warrants a temporary stay.
defendant_argument: Harris County argues that the Uplift Harris program is a constitutional exercise of its authority, potentially qualifying as either permissible under existing Gift Clauses or as an authorized 'economic development' program under Article III, Section 52-a of the Texas Constitution.
decision_outcome: The court granted the State's motion for temporary relief, ordering Harris County to cease distributing funds under the Uplift Harris program until further order of the Court, citing serious constitutional doubts.

title: Texas Department of Transportation v. Self
description: Whether the Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT) is immune from liability for negligence or inverse condemnation after its subcontractor cut down trees on private property outside the State's right-of-way easement.
plaintiff_argument: The Selfs argued that TxDOT was negligent because the subcontractor's employees were TxDOT employees or TxDOT employees operated the equipment, and that TxDOT was liable for inverse condemnation because it intentionally directed the destruction of trees on their property for public use.
defendant_argument: TxDOT argued it was immune from the negligence claim as the subcontractor's employees were not its employees and its employees did not operate the equipment, and that it lacked the requisite intent for inverse condemnation, believing the trees were within its right-of-way.
decision_outcome: The court dismissed the negligence cause of action against TxDOT but remanded the inverse condemnation cause of action to the trial court for further proceedings.

title: Hogan v. Southern Methodist University
description: Whether the application of the Pandemic Liability Protection Act to Hogan's breach-of-contract claim violates the retroactivity clause in article I, section 16 of the Texas Constitution, with Hogan arguing it does and SMU arguing it does not.
plaintiff_argument: Hogan argued that the Pandemic Liability Protection Act (PLPA) retroactively withdrew his right to hold SMU liable for breaking its promise of in-person education, violating Article I, Section 16's prohibition on "retroactive law[s]" and impairing his pre-existing contract remedies, asserting he had a settled expectation of recovery for damages.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that the PLPA protects schools from monetary liability for altering activities in response to the pandemic, and its application does not violate the retroactivity clause because there was no "settled expectation" for Hogan to recover damages under the novel circumstances, and the common law impossibility doctrine would have limited SMU's liability anyway.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that the application of the Pandemic Liability Protection Act to Hogan's breach-of-contract claim does not violate the retroactivity clause in article I, section 16 of the Texas Constitution.

title: Texas v. Loe
description: Whether the Legislature's prohibition of certain medical treatments for children suffering from gender dysphoria violates the Texas Constitution by infringing upon parental rights, physicians' occupational freedom, and by discriminating against transgender adolescents.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argue that Senate Bill 14 (S.B. 14) is unconstitutional as it infringes on parents' fundamental right to make medical decisions for their children, violates Texas physicians' right of occupational freedom, and discriminates against transgender adolescents based on sex, sex stereotypes, and transgender status.
defendant_argument: The defendants argue that S.B. 14 is facially constitutional and that the plaintiffs' claims are invalid, asserting the Legislature's authority to regulate medical practice and protect children's health and welfare.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the trial court's temporary injunction order, concluding that the plaintiffs failed to establish a probable right to relief on any of their three asserted constitutional violations.

title: City of Dallas v. Employees Retirement Fund of City of Dallas
description: Whether a city ordinance can grant a third party, like the Employees' Retirement Fund board, a perpetual veto right over future lawmaking related to the fund, with the plaintiff (Fund) arguing it can due to trust law, and the defendant (City) arguing it cannot due to unconstitutional delegation of lawmaking authority.
plaintiff_argument: The Employees' Retirement Fund (Fund) argued that Chapter 40A, which governs the Fund, is a 'trust document' and cannot be amended without the board's consent, relying on trust law principles to invalidate the City's term limits ordinance.
defendant_argument: The City of Dallas (City) argued that the term limits ordinance amended Chapter 8, not Chapter 40A, and even if it did amend Chapter 40A, the board's veto power in § 40A-35(a) is unenforceable because it unconstitutionally delegates lawmaking authority.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas reversed the court of appeals' judgment, holding that the board's veto power is unenforceable as an unconstitutional delegation of legislative authority, and remanded the case for further proceedings on the necessity of voter approval for amendments.

title: Becerra v. State
description: Whether an alternate juror's presence and participation in jury deliberations, including voting, violates the constitutional right to a twelve-person jury or statutory prohibitions against unauthorized persons influencing deliberations, and if so, what standard for harm applies.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the alternate juror's participation violated his constitutional right to a twelve-person jury and statutory provisions regarding external influence, therefore a new trial should be granted, or at least a presumption of harm should be applied.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that the alternate juror's presence did not violate jury composition rules, or if it did, the error was harmless, and any presumption of harm should be rebutted or not applied.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that the alternate juror's presence did not violate constitutional jury composition requirements but did violate statutory prohibitions against unauthorized persons in deliberations, and remanded the case to the court of appeals to conduct a statutory harm analysis using a non-constitutional error standard.

title: Hensley v. State Commission on Judicial Conduct
description: Whether Judge Dianne Hensley was required to exhaust her administrative remedies before suing the State Commission on Judicial Conduct for alleged violations of the Texas Religious Freedom Restoration Act.
plaintiff_argument: Judge Hensley argued she was not required to exhaust administrative remedies because the Special Court of Review (SCR) could not provide the specific relief she sought (damages, declaratory relief, injunction), and her suit was not a collateral attack on the warning.
defendant_argument: The State Commission on Judicial Conduct argued that Judge Hensley was required to exhaust administrative remedies, asserting that it was a jurisdictional prerequisite and the administrative process could have potentially resolved or mooted her claims.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that Judge Dianne Hensley was not required to exhaust her administrative remedies before filing her lawsuit against the State Commission on Judicial Conduct.

title: Zurawski v. Texas
description: Whether Texas's abortion laws, specifically the Human Life Protection Act, are constitutional and how they should be interpreted regarding life-saving abortions, with plaintiffs arguing for a broader interpretation to include "unsafe pregnancies" and "fetal conditions unlikely to sustain life," and the state arguing the law permits abortions for life-threatening physical conditions based on reasonable medical judgment without requiring imminent death.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that Texas's abortion laws were unclear and unconstitutional, requesting a broader interpretation to permit abortions for "unsafe" pregnancies, conditions untreatable during pregnancy, and fetal conditions where the child is unlikely to survive, fearing legal consequences for physicians.
defendant_argument: The State argued that the Human Life Protection Act already permits life-saving abortions when a woman faces a life-threatening physical condition, that the law is constitutional, and that physicians can intervene before death or serious physical impairment are imminent.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas vacated the trial court's temporary injunction, ruling that the trial court's order incorrectly interpreted the Human Life Protection Act and lacked constitutional justification to depart from the law's text.

title: Harris County v. Texas
description: Whether Senate Bill 1750 (SB 1750) is an unconstitutional local law under the Texas Constitution, where the plaintiff argues it will cause imminent and irreparable harm to election administration if enforced, and the defendant argues it should be enforced.
plaintiff_argument: Harris County argues that SB 1750 is an unconstitutional local law that, if implemented, would cause immense inefficiencies, disorganization, confusion, office instability, increased costs, and disrupt the upcoming November 2023 election, leading to irreparable injury.
defendant_argument: The State of Texas, Angela Colmenero (Interim Attorney General), and Jane Nelson (Texas Secretary of State) implicitly argue for the constitutionality and enforcement of SB 1750.
decision_outcome: The court granted Harris County's request for a temporary injunction, enjoining the State Officer Defendants from taking any actions to enforce SB 1750 and from refusing to recognize the Harris County Elections Administrator's Office.

title: Ex parte Sheffield
description: Whether speedy trial claims are cognizable in pretrial habeas corpus when a trial is indefinitely postponed, with the plaintiff arguing yes under the Ex parte Perry rule and the defendant arguing no, as it would undermine the right and other remedies exist.
plaintiff_argument: Appellant Kevin Dale Sheffield argued that the indefinite delay in jury trials due to COVID-19 emergency orders violated his right to a speedy trial, and this claim was cognizable in pretrial habeas corpus under the Ex parte Perry rule because his right to a timely disposition would otherwise be undermined.
defendant_argument: The State Prosecuting Attorney's Office (SPA) argued that speedy trial claims are not cognizable in pretrial habeas corpus, as allowing such claims through interlocutory appeal would inject appellate delay and undermine the speedy trial right; instead, mandamus or post-trial appeal are the proper remedies.
decision_outcome: The Court of Criminal Appeals reversed the court of appeals' judgment, holding that speedy trial claims are not cognizable in pretrial habeas corpus and the Perry rule does not apply to such claims, because pretrial habeas would undermine, not vindicate, the speedy trial right.

title: Hotze v. Turner
description: Whether a city's "primacy clause," which makes a citizen-initiated charter amendment ineffective unless it receives more votes than a council-initiated amendment, violates state law requiring charter amendments to be adopted by a simple majority vote and become effective upon declaration, and whether the two voter-approved amendments can be harmonized.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, Bruce R. Hotze, argued that the City's primacy clause violates state law (Tex. Loc. Gov't Code § 9.005(a)) by imposing a higher voting threshold for the citizen-initiated amendment (Proposition 2) to take effect, and that the City cannot indefinitely delay its effectiveness. He also argued that the two voter-approved amendments could be harmonized.
defendant_argument: The defendants, Sylvester Turner, Mayor, and the City of Houston, argued that the primacy clause was valid and did not conflict with state law because state law only governs the adoption of amendments, not their effectiveness, which the City claims is within its discretion. They also contended that the two amendments irreconcilably conflicted, making Proposition 2 ineffective under the City's conflict-resolution provision.
decision_outcome: The Texas Supreme Court reversed the court of appeals' judgment, ruling that the City's primacy clause is void for conflicting with state law that requires charter amendments to be adopted by a majority vote and take effect upon official declaration. The case was remanded to the trial court to determine how the two voter-approved amendments could be harmonized.

title: City of Houston v. Houston Proffesional Fire Fighters Association
description: Whether Chapter 174's judicial-enforcement provisions violate the Texas Constitution's separation of powers and governmental immunity, and whether Chapter 174 preempts a local pay-parity amendment regarding firefighter compensation.
plaintiff_argument: The Houston Professional Fire Fighters' Association argued that Chapter 174's judicial enforcement provisions are constitutional and waive governmental immunity, and that Chapter 174 does not preempt the local pay-parity amendment.
defendant_argument: The City of Houston argued that Chapter 174's judicial enforcement provisions are unconstitutional, that it retains governmental immunity, and, joined by the Houston Police Officers' Union, that Chapter 174 does preempt the local pay-parity amendment.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed that Chapter 174's judicial-enforcement provisions are constitutional and waive the City's governmental immunity, but reversed the lower court on the preemption issue, holding that Chapter 174 preempts the local pay-parity amendment.

title: The Lilith Fund for Reproductive Equity v. Dickson
description: Whether statements made by an anti-abortion advocate characterizing abortion as murder and abortion providers as criminal organizations constitute legally defamatory falsehoods or protected free speech.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that the defendant's statements were legally defamatory because they falsely characterized abortion as murder and labeled the plaintiffs as 'criminal organizations' when abortion was legal under Texas law at the time.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that his statements were protected opinions about abortion law, made in pursuit of changing that law, and therefore constituted protected speech under the United States and Texas Constitutions, not defamation.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that the challenged statements were protected opinion about abortion law and free speech, and therefore affirmed the dismissal of the defamation suit against the defendant.

title: Virlar v. Puente
description: Whether Chapter 33 of the Civil Practice and Remedies Code requires a credit for a family member's settlement against a judgment and whether the Texas Medical Liability Act (TMLA) mandates periodic payments for future medical expenses.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued against crediting her daughter's settlement, claiming it would violate the Open Courts provision, and contended that periodic payments for future medical expenses were not properly requested or supported by evidence.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that Chapter 33 required a credit for the daughter's settlement and that the TMLA mandated periodic payments for future medical expenses.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the judgment in part, holding that the trial court improperly denied a settlement credit for the daughter's settlement and an award of periodic payments for future medical expenses, and remanded the case for further proceedings.

title: State v. Aktiengesellschaft
description: Whether the Governor's appointment of substitute justices to hear a case involving the State, after original justices recused, violates due process or creates an appearance of partiality.
plaintiff_argument: The State (through the Attorney General) argues that the Governor's appointment of substitute justices is a legitimate statutory process and does not create an inherent bias or violate due process, as the Governor is distinct from the named plaintiff.
defendant_argument: Volkswagen and Audi (Respondents) argue that the Governor's appointments effectively make the State "judge of its own cause," violating constitutional due process and ethical standards, and creating an appearance of impropriety.
decision_outcome: The court denied the Respondents' requests, affirming that the Governor's appointment of substitute justices does not violate due process or ethical rules, allowing the cases to proceed with the appointed justices.

title: Nath v. Texas Children's Hospital
description: Whether Dr. Rahul Nath is entitled to a jury finding on the reasonableness and necessity of attorney's fees imposed as sanctions, with Dr. Nath arguing for this constitutional right and the hospital and college arguing it is barred by prior rulings or waiver.
plaintiff_argument: Dr. Nath argued he has a constitutional right to a jury trial to determine the reasonableness and necessity of the attorney's fees sanctions repeatedly assessed against him.
defendant_argument: Texas Children's Hospital and Baylor College of Medicine contended that Dr. Nath's request for a jury trial on attorney's fees was barred by the law-of-the-case doctrine or that he had waived the issue.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas denied Dr. Nath's petition for review, thereby upholding the lower court's decision to impose sanctions without a jury trial on the attorney's fees.

title: Texas Department of State Health Services v. Crown Distributing LLC
description: Whether the Texas Constitution's due-course clause protects the interest of manufacturing and processing smokable hemp products.
plaintiff_argument: The state's ban on manufacturing and processing smokable hemp products violates the Texas Constitution's due-course clause, infringing on their economic liberty to work in a business that was, or became, lawful.
defendant_argument: The due-course clause does not protect the Hemp Companies' interest because manufacturing smokable hemp products is not a 'lawful calling' or a 'vested right' as these products were previously illegal.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the trial court's judgment, ruling that the manufacturing and processing of smokable hemp products is not an interest substantively protected by the Texas Constitution's due-course clause.

title: Abbott v. Mexican American Legislative Caucus
description: Whether the recently enacted Texas reapportionment laws (H.B. 1 and S.B. 4) violate Article III, Sections 26 (county-line rule) and 28 (timing) of the Texas Constitution, and whether the plaintiffs' claims are barred by jurisdictional issues such as standing, mootness, and sovereign immunity.
plaintiff_argument: The Mexican American Legislative Caucus (MALC) argued H.B. 1 violates the county-line rule (Section 26). The Gutierrez Plaintiffs argued H.B. 1 violates Section 26 and both H.B. 1 and S.B. 4 violate Section 28 by being enacted during a special session instead of the first regular session after the census, infringing a state senator's right to a four-year term.
defendant_argument: The State defendants argued that the claims were moot, plaintiffs lacked standing, and claims were barred by sovereign immunity. They contended H.B. 1 complies with Section 26, and Section 28 does not prohibit reapportionment at other times, especially given the census delay.
decision_outcome: The court dismissed MALC's claims and the Gutierrez Plaintiffs' Section 28 claim for lack of jurisdiction due to associational standing and sovereign immunity respectively, but reversed the dismissal of the Gutierrez Plaintiffs' Section 26 claim and remanded it for repleading against a proper defendant.

title: Fire Protection Service v. Survitec Survival Products
description: Whether the application of the Fair Practices of Equipment Manufacturers, Distributors, Wholesalers, and Dealers Act to a pre-existing at-will agreement violates the retroactivity clause in Article I, Section 16 of the Texas Constitution.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, Fire Protection Service, Inc. (FPS), argued that the Act's application does not violate the retroactivity clause and that Survitec's constitutional challenge should fail.
defendant_argument: The defendant, Survitec Survival Products, Inc., argued that applying the Act to its preexisting at-will agreement unconstitutionally eliminated its right to terminate the relationship without cause.
decision_outcome: The court concluded that the application of the Act to the parties' agreement does not violate the constitutional prohibition against retroactive laws in Article I, Section 16 of the Texas Constitution.

title: City of Baytown v. Schrock
description: Whether the City of Baytown's utility enforcement actions, which included refusing service and placing a lien due to unpaid bills, constituted a regulatory taking of Alan Schrock's property under the Texas or United States Constitution.
plaintiff_argument: Schrock claimed the city's wrongful withholding of utility service to collect payment resulted in loss of a tenant and property disrepair, constituting a taking in violation of the Texas or United States Constitution.
defendant_argument: The City argued its enforcement actions did not show an intentional taking or damage for public use necessary to establish a constitutional right to compensation, asserting governmental immunity.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the court of appeals' judgment and reinstated the trial court's directed verdict, ruling that the City's utility enforcement actions did not establish a regulatory taking of private property as a matter of law.

title: State v. Stephens
description: Whether the Attorney General (AG) has independent constitutional authority to unilaterally prosecute election law offenses under Section 273.021 of the Texas Election Code, or if such authority violates the Texas Constitution's Separation of Powers Clause.
plaintiff_argument: Stephens argued that the Attorney General lacked independent authority to prosecute election law offenses, and that Section 273.021 of the Texas Election Code, which grants such authority, violates the Texas Constitution's Separation of Powers Clause.
defendant_argument: The Attorney General (representing the State) argued that the AG does possess the constitutional authority to prosecute election law offenses independently and that Stephens lacked standing to challenge who represented the State in her prosecution.
decision_outcome: The Court denied the Attorney General's motion for rehearing, thereby affirming its original decision that Section 273.021 of the Texas Election Code, authorizing the AG to prosecute election law offenses, violates the Texas Constitution's Separation of Powers Clause, leading to the dismissal of Stephens's indictment.

title: In re Abbott
description: Whether the Texas House of Representatives has the authority to physically compel the attendance of absent members to achieve a quorum, or if its power to "compel attendance" is limited to persuasion and dialogue.
plaintiff_argument: The House's power to "compel the attendance of absent members" under Article III, Section 10 of the Texas Constitution authorizes only persuasion and dialogue, not physical compulsion or arrest. They sought an injunction to prevent their arrest for breaking quorum.
defendant_argument: The Texas Constitution, Article III, Section 10, empowers the House of Representatives to physically compel the attendance of absent members, including by arrest and detention, to achieve a quorum and conduct legislative business, consistent with historical understanding and legislative rules.
decision_outcome: The court concluded that the Texas Constitution empowers the House of Representatives to physically compel the attendance of absent members and directed the district court to withdraw its temporary restraining order (TRO) that had prohibited such actions.

title: Odyssey 2020 Academy, Inc. v. Galveston Central Appraisal District
description: Whether privately owned property leased and used by a public charter school, which a state statute considers "public property for all purposes," is exempt from ad valorem taxes under the Texas Constitution's requirement of actual public ownership.
plaintiff_argument: Odyssey argues that the property it leases for its public charter school is "public property for all purposes" under Education Code section 12.128(a), making it eligible for tax exemption under various constitutional and statutory provisions, irrespective of fee ownership.
defendant_argument: The District argues that the Texas Constitution requires actual public ownership for a tax exemption, which the privately owned, leased property lacks, and a statute cannot recharacterize private property as publicly owned for tax exemption purposes.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court of Texas affirmed the lower court's judgment, ruling that Odyssey 2020 Academy, Inc. is not entitled to an exemption from ad valorem taxes on the privately owned property it leases.

title: Texas State University v. Villarreal
description: Whether a state university's dismissal of a student for poor academic performance implicates a liberty or property interest protected by the Texas Constitution's guarantee of due course of law, where the plaintiff argues it does and the defendant argues it does not.
plaintiff_argument: Ivan Villarreal contended that his academic dismissal violated his liberty and property rights under the Texas Constitution's due course of law, arguing that the university mishandled a cheating investigation that contributed to his low grades and dismissal, and that his continued graduate education was a protected interest.
defendant_argument: Texas Southern University argued that academic dismissal for poor performance does not implicate a protected liberty interest, nor does the Texas Constitution recognize higher education as a fundamental right, and that they provided adequate procedural due course of law for Villarreal's dismissal.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the court of appeals' judgment regarding Villarreal's constitutional claims, ruling that academic dismissal does not implicate a protected liberty interest and that the procedures followed were constitutionally adequate, thereby dismissing the case.

title: Texas Propane Gas Association v. City of Houston
description: Whether civil courts have subject-matter jurisdiction over the Texas Propane Gas Association's (TPGA) preemption claim challenging City of Houston ordinances that carry criminal penalties, and whether TPGA has standing to challenge the city's liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) regulations en masse.
plaintiff_argument: The Texas Propane Gas Association (TPGA) argued that the City of Houston's LPG regulations are preempted by state law, that civil courts have jurisdiction over their preemption claim, and that TPGA has standing to challenge all such regulations.
defendant_argument: The City of Houston argued that civil courts lack subject-matter jurisdiction because its LPG regulations carry criminal penalties, and that TPGA lacks standing to challenge all of the City's regulations without showing specific injury for each one.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the court of appeals' judgment, ruling that civil courts have subject-matter jurisdiction over TPGA's preemption claim and that TPGA has standing to challenge the city's LPG regulations; the case was remanded to the trial court for further proceedings on the merits.

title: Dulin v. State
description: Whether the 'time payment fee' assessed against a defendant is valid, with the plaintiff arguing it is unconstitutional under separation of powers and the defendant arguing it is either prematurely assessed or a legitimate court cost.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the $22.50 time payment fee violated the principle of separation of powers and should be struck from the court costs.
defendant_argument: The defendant (State) argued that the time payment fee was prematurely assessed, or alternatively, that prior precedent (*Salinas*) on separation of powers was wrongly decided and the fee is a legitimate court cost.
decision_outcome: The Court ruled that the time payment fee was prematurely assessed because an ongoing appeal suspends the obligation to pay court costs, vacating the lower court's judgment and remanding the case for further proceedings.

title: Wren v. Columbia St. Mary’s Hospital
description: Whether Wisconsin Statute § 895.4801, which grants immunity to health care professionals during the COVID-19 pandemic, facially violates the constitutional right to a jury trial under Article I, Section 5 of the Wisconsin Constitution, with the plaintiff arguing it infringes this fundamental right and the defendant arguing the legislature has the power to suspend common law causes of action, thereby nullifying the jury trial right.
plaintiff_argument: Wren argued that § 895.4801 is unconstitutional because it violates the right to a jury trial under Article I, Section 5 of the Wisconsin Constitution, contending it burdens a fundamental right and is not narrowly tailored.
defendant_argument: Columbia St. Mary's argued that Article XIV, Section 13 of the Wisconsin Constitution empowers the legislature to alter or suspend common law causes of action, and by suspending the medical malpractice cause of action, the right to a jury trial does not attach.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the court of appeals' decision, ruling that WIS. STAT. § 895.4801 does not facially violate the jury trial right under Article I, Section 5 of the Wisconsin Constitution because when a cause of action is abrogated by the legislature, the right to a jury trial does not attach.

title: State v. Ramirez
description: Whether Luis A. Ramirez's constitutional right to a speedy trial was violated due to a 46-month delay from charges being filed to conviction.
plaintiff_argument: The State argued that Ramirez's speedy trial right was not violated, citing valid and neutral reasons for the delays, Ramirez's delay in asserting his right, and his failure to prove particularized prejudice.
defendant_argument: Ramirez contended that the 46-month delay in bringing him to trial violated his constitutional speedy trial right, emphasizing the extreme length of the delay and prejudice suffered.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court reversed the court of appeals' decision, concluding that Ramirez's constitutional right to a speedy trial was not violated under the circumstances of this case.

title: Wisconsin State Legislature v. Wisconsin Department of Public Instruction
description: Whether the governor impermissibly exercised his partial veto power on a non-appropriation bill (S.B. 971) and whether the Joint Committee on Finance (JCF) improperly withheld funds earmarked for literacy programs from the Department of Public Instruction (DPI).
plaintiff_argument: The Wisconsin State Legislature argued that S.B. 971 was not an appropriation bill and thus not subject to the governor's partial veto authority, making the veto unconstitutional. They also contended that the JCF properly withheld funds from DPI.
defendant_argument: The governor and DPI argued that S.B. 971 was an appropriation bill, subject to the governor's partial veto power, and counterclaimed that the JCF improperly withheld $50 million earmarked for DPI's literacy programs.
decision_outcome: The Supreme Court held that the governor's partial veto of S.B. 971 was unconstitutional because it was not an appropriation bill, meaning S.B. 971 as passed by the legislature is the law. The court also affirmed that the Joint Committee on Finance did not improperly withhold funds from the Department of Public Instruction.
