title: Josh Kaul v. Wisconsin State Legislature
description: Whether a state law requiring legislative approval for the Department of Justice to settle civil enforcement actions and cases brought at the request of executive-branch agencies violates the Wisconsin Constitution's separation of powers.
plaintiff_argument: The Department of Justice (DOJ) argues that settling these specific categories of cases constitutes core executive power, which the Legislature cannot constitutionally interfere with.
defendant_argument: The Legislature contends it has constitutional interests related to the 'power of the purse' (state revenue from settlements) and 'policymaking' that justify its requirement for settlement approval.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the decision of the court of appeals, ruling that the statute requiring legislative approval for the Department of Justice to settle specific civil cases is unconstitutional as it violates the Wisconsin Constitution's separation of powers.

title: Planned Parenthood v. Urmanski
description: Whether Planned Parenthood's original action challenging abortion statutes in Wisconsin should proceed, or if it is moot due to a concurrent statutory challenge ruling.
plaintiff_argument: Planned Parenthood filed an original action to challenge abortion-related statutes and establish a constitutional right to abortion, arguing the case should be heard.
defendant_argument: The District Attorneys implicitly argued that the case should not proceed or was rendered moot by other legal decisions.
decision_outcome: The court dismissed the original action and all pending motions as moot, citing its concurrent decision in Kaul v. Urmanski.

title: Kaul v. Urmanski
description: Whether Wisconsin Statute § 940.04(1), a 19th-century law criminalizing the intentional destruction of an unborn child, has been impliedly repealed by subsequent comprehensive legislation regulating abortion, and therefore no longer bans abortion.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that § 940.04(1) does not ban abortion because it either does not apply to abortion at all, or has been impliedly repealed by numerous subsequent statutes.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that the plain language of § 940.04(1) bans nearly all abortions and that the legislature has not impliedly repealed that prohibition.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the circuit court's judgment, ruling that Wisconsin Statute § 940.04(1) has been impliedly repealed as to abortion and therefore does not prohibit abortion in the state.

title: Evers v. Marklein
description: Whether statutes empowering the Joint Committee for Review of Administrative Rules (JCRAR) to pause, object to, or suspend administrative rules are unconstitutional legislative vetoes, with petitioners arguing these actions violate bicameralism and presentment, and respondents contending they are permissible extensions of legislative power.
plaintiff_argument: The Governor argued that the challenged statutes amount to unconstitutional legislative vetoes because JCRAR's actions alter legal rights and duties outside the legislative branch without fulfilling the bicameralism and presentment requirements of the Wisconsin Constitution.
defendant_argument: The Legislature argued that the challenged statutes are permissible because rulemaking is an appropriate extension of legislative power, and that JCRAR's rule suspensions and objections do not require bicameralism and presentment as they differ from legislation.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that Wisconsin Statutes §§ 227.19(5)(c), (d), (dm) and 227.26(2)(d), (im), which empower JCRAR to pause, object to, and suspend administrative rules, are facially unconstitutional because they violate the Wisconsin Constitution's bicameralism and presentment requirements.

title: LeMieux v. Evers
description: Whether Governor Tony Evers exceeded his partial veto authority under Article V, Section 10(1) of the Wisconsin Constitution by striking words and digits from the 2023-25 biennial budget bill to extend an education revenue limit increase from two fiscal years to 402 fiscal years.
plaintiff_argument: The petitioners argued that the governor's partial vetoes violated Article V, Section 10(1)(b) because extending a two-year duration to 402 years is not vetoing "in part," and violated Section 10(1)(c) by striking digits to create new numbers.
defendant_argument: The governor (respondent) argued that his partial vetoes were constitutional, satisfying existing deletion-veto principles, and that Section 10(1)(c) does not prohibit striking digits to create new numbers.
decision_outcome: The court ruled against the petitioners, upholding Governor Evers' partial vetoes as consistent with Article V Sections 10(1)(b) and (c) of the Wisconsin Constitution.

title: Brown v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether Kenneth Brown has standing to seek judicial review of a Wisconsin Elections Commission (WEC) decision regarding in-person absentee voting procedures, with Brown arguing he has standing due to a legal right to challenge unlawful election conduct, and WEC arguing he does not have standing because he suffered no direct personal injury.
plaintiff_argument: Brown contended that the in-person absentee voting procedures implemented by the Racine City Clerk violated the law and that WEC's decision not to take corrective action injured his statutory right to have local election officials comply with the law, thereby granting him standing to seek judicial review.
defendant_argument: The Wisconsin Elections Commission (WEC) argued that Kenneth Brown did not have standing to seek judicial review because he failed to demonstrate that WEC's decision caused him a legally recognized personal injury.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the circuit court's decision, ruling that Kenneth Brown lacked standing to seek judicial review of the Wisconsin Elections Commission's decision, and remanded the case with instructions to dismiss.

title: State v. Hoyle
description: Whether the prosecutor's repeated description of the State's evidence as "uncontroverted" during closing arguments violated the defendant's Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination by adversely commenting on his decision not to testify.
plaintiff_argument: The State (prosecution) argued that its comments describing the evidence as "uncontroverted" were not intended to comment on the defendant's silence, but rather to remind the jury to focus solely on the evidence presented at trial and not to speculate about other possible evidence, and therefore did not violate the Fifth Amendment.
defendant_argument: Tomas Jaymitchell Hoyle (defendant) argued that the prosecutor's repeated characterization of the evidence as "uncontroverted" was an improper comment on his decision not to testify, as only he could have controverted the alleged victim's testimony, thus violating his Fifth Amendment rights.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the decision of the court of appeals, finding that the prosecutor's comments did not violate the defendant's Fifth Amendment rights, and remanded the case to the court of appeals for consideration of unresolved issues.

title: Priorities USA v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether to overrule the Court's previous holding in Teigen v. Wisconsin Elections Commission that Wis. Stat. § 6.87 precludes the use of secure drop boxes for the return of absentee ballots to municipal clerks.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that Teigen was wrongly decided and that Wis. Stat. § 6.87(4)(b)1 allows the use of ballot drop boxes.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that Teigen was correctly decided and that its statutory interpretation, which banned ballot drop boxes, should be reaffirmed.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the circuit court's dismissal of the petitioners' claim, ruling that Wis. Stat. § 6.87(4)(b)1 allows the use of ballot drop boxes and overruling the previous Teigen decision.

title: Catholic Charities Bureau, Inc. v. State
description: Whether Catholic Charities Bureau, Inc. and its sub-entities are exempt from paying unemployment tax under Wisconsin's Unemployment Compensation Act because they are operated primarily for religious purposes.
plaintiff_argument: Catholic Charities Bureau, Inc. (CCB) and its sub-entities argue they are exempt from unemployment tax because they are operated primarily for religious purposes, motivated by religious principles, and that a contrary interpretation violates the First Amendment.
defendant_argument: LIRC argues that the organization's actual activities, not its motivations, are paramount in determining if it is operated primarily for religious purposes, and its interpretation does not violate the First Amendment.
decision_outcome: The decision of the court of appeals is affirmed, meaning CCB and its sub-entities are not exempt from unemployment tax.

title: Wisconsin Property Taxpayers, Inc. v. Town of Buchanan
description: Whether a municipal transportation utility fee (TUF) is a property tax subject to statutory levy limits and uniformity requirements, or a special tax exempt from such regulations.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff argued that the Transportation Utility Fee (TUF) is a property tax subject to municipal levy limits and other statutory property tax requirements, demanding that revenue be offset by general property tax levies and comply with the Uniformity Clause.
defendant_argument: The defendant argued that Wis. Stat. § 66.0827 authorizes the TUF as a special tax, exempt from general property tax levy limits and uniformity requirements, and that it functions as a separate governmental unit.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the circuit court's judgment, ruling that the Town of Buchanan's Transportation Utility Fee (TUF) is a property tax subject to municipal levy limits, which the Town unlawfully exceeded without voter approval.

title: Teigen v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether the Wisconsin Elections Commission's guidance authorizing ballot drop boxes and allowing agents to return absentee ballots is lawful under state election statutes.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs argued that the Wisconsin Elections Commission's memos are invalid as unpromulgated administrative rules and because state statutes require absentee ballots to be personally mailed or delivered by the voter to the municipal clerk, thereby prohibiting drop boxes and agent delivery.
defendant_argument: The defendants argued that WEC's memos were valid guidance, not administrative rules, and that state statutes do not explicitly prohibit ballot drop boxes or agent delivery for absentee ballots, implying such methods are permissible.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the circuit court's judgment, declaring WEC's guidance memos authorizing ballot drop boxes and agent delivery of absentee ballots as invalid because they conflict with Wisconsin election statutes.

title: Clarke v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether the existing state legislative maps violate Article IV, Sections 4 and 5 of the Wisconsin Constitution by not consisting of "contiguous territory", with petitioners arguing the districts are non-contiguous and respondents arguing they comply with contiguity requirements.
plaintiff_argument: The current legislative districts are non-contiguous, violating Article IV, Sections 4 and 5 of the Wisconsin Constitution, and their adoption process also violated the separation of powers. They seek to enjoin the use of current maps and order new remedial maps.
defendant_argument: The current state legislative districts comply with the Wisconsin Constitution's contiguity requirements, and the petitioners' claims are barred by various legal defenses such as laches, preclusion, and estoppel.
decision_outcome: The court held that the current state legislative districts violate the Wisconsin Constitution's contiguity requirements by containing separate, detached territory, and therefore enjoined the Wisconsin Elections Commission from using these maps in future elections.

title: Johnson v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether the court should grant relief from its prior decision and order concerning the adoption of congressional district maps.
plaintiff_argument: The intervenors-petitioners, Lisa Hunter et al., argued that the court should grant their motion for relief from the decision and order entered on March 3, 2022, regarding congressional district maps.
defendant_argument: Various respondents, including the Wisconsin Elections Commission, petitioners Billie Johnson et al., and the Wisconsin Legislature, argued that the motion for relief from judgment should be denied.
decision_outcome: The court granted the motion for leave to file a reply but denied the motion for relief from judgment.

title: State v. Johnson
description: Whether the Shiffra procedure, which allows criminal defendants to obtain in camera review of a victim's privately held, privileged health records, should be overturned.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff (State) and T.A.J. (the victim/appellant) argued that the Shiffra procedure was wrongly decided, unworkable, and its rationale undermined by subsequent legal developments, thus requesting it be overturned.
defendant_argument: The defendant (Johnson) argued for the continuation of the Shiffra procedure to allow access to the victim's mental health and counseling records for his defense.
decision_outcome: The court overturned the Shiffra procedure, reversing the court of appeals' decision and instructing the circuit court to deny Johnson's motion for in camera review of T.A.J.'s privately held, privileged mental health treatment records.

title: Wisconsin Justice Initiative,  Inc. v. Wisconsin Elections Commission
description: Whether Marsy's Law was adopted in violation of the Wisconsin Constitution's amendment process, with the plaintiff arguing the ballot question was misleading and multiple amendments should have been submitted, and the defendant arguing the submission process was constitutionally sound.
plaintiff_argument: The ballot question for Marsy's Law failed to disclose "every essential" of the proposed amendment, misled voters by not mentioning its impact on criminal defendants' rights, and should have been submitted as multiple amendments.
defendant_argument: Marsy's Law was properly submitted as a single amendment, and the ballot question was not fundamentally counterfactual, satisfying constitutional requirements and giving broad discretion to the legislature.
decision_outcome: The Wisconsin Supreme Court reversed the circuit court's judgment, concluding that Marsy's Law was validly ratified.

title: Becker v. Dane Cnty.
description: Whether the court should reconsider its previous denial of a nondelegation challenge, with petitioners arguing the court's original interpretation of historical statutes misconstrued the delegation of public health authority to elected versus unelected officials, and the court maintaining its interpretation was sound and the motion for reconsideration did not meet standards.
plaintiff_argument: The motion for reconsideration should be granted because the court's prior ruling relied on a novel 'text-and-history' approach and misconstrued an 1849 statute, erroneously assuming that public health rulemaking authority could be delegated to unelected bureaucrats, thereby undermining the constitutional separation of powers.
defendant_argument: The motion for reconsideration should be denied because it does not meet the court's standards, and the court's original decision correctly applied the nondelegation principle based on an appropriate historical understanding of statutory authority.
decision_outcome: The court denied the petitioners' motion for reconsideration without costs.

title: State v. Moeser
description: Whether the warrant compelling a blood draw was constitutionally defective because the affiant was not placed under oath or affirmation.
plaintiff_argument: The State argued that the oath or affirmation requirement was met because the affidavit's language and the officer's actions demonstrated an intention to be under oath, satisfying the substance of the constitutional requirement.
defendant_argument: Moeser argued that the warrant was constitutionally defective because the affiant, Sergeant Brown, was not formally placed under an oral oath or affirmation when signing the affidavit, failing to meet Fourth Amendment requirements.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the decision of the court of appeals, which had affirmed the circuit court's denial of Moeser's motion to suppress evidence.

title: Doe v. Madison Metropolitan School District
description: Whether parents challenging a school district's gender identity policy can proceed with total anonymity, specifically withholding their identities from opposing counsel, given the public nature of judicial proceedings and the court's discretion in balancing privacy concerns against litigation needs.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiffs (parents) argued that the school district's policy infringes on their constitutional right to direct their children's upbringing and education, and that their identities should remain sealed, even from opposing counsel, due to risks of harassment and retaliation.
defendant_argument: The Madison Metropolitan School District (MMSD) argued that parents' identities are relevant for proper litigation, including assessing potential conflicts of interest and developing legal defenses, and that attorneys are duty-bound to maintain confidentiality.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the circuit court's decision, which allowed the parents to proceed anonymously to the public but required them to disclose their identities to the school district's attorneys, and remanded the case for further proceedings without ruling on the merits of the underlying policy.

title: State ex rel. Kaul v. Prehn
description: Whether Frederick Prehn lawfully retains his position on the Wisconsin Board of Natural Resources as a holdover after his term expired, and whether the Governor can remove him without cause.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, the State, argues that Frederick Prehn's term on the DNR Board expired, creating a vacancy, and that he is unlawfully holding office and can be removed by the Governor at will without cause.
defendant_argument: The defendant, Frederick Prehn, argues that no vacancy occurred when his term expired, he can lawfully remain on the DNR Board as a holdover until a successor is confirmed, and he is entitled to "for cause" protection, preventing his removal without cause.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the circuit court's dismissal of the State's complaint, ruling that Frederick Prehn lawfully retains his position on the DNR Board as a holdover and can only be removed for cause, not at the Governor's pleasure.

title: James v. Heinrich
description: Whether a local health officer has the statutory authority to close schools for in-person instruction during a pandemic and whether such an order violates fundamental rights like the free exercise of religion.
plaintiff_argument: The local health officer exceeded her statutory authority by closing schools, and the order violated fundamental rights to free exercise of religion and parental rights to direct children's education.
defendant_argument: The local health officer had statutory authority under Wis. Stat. § 252.03 to issue school closure orders, and the order was constitutional.
decision_outcome: The court ruled that the local health officer lacked statutory authority to close schools and that her order infringed the petitioners' fundamental right to the free exercise of religion, thus vacating the order restricting in-person instruction.

title: State v. VanBeek
description: Whether a consensual encounter becomes an unconstitutional seizure under the Fourth Amendment when an officer requests and takes an individual's driver's license to the officer's squad car without reasonable suspicion, with the plaintiff arguing it does and the defendant arguing it does not, or that there was reasonable suspicion.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, Heather VanBeek, argued that the officer's demand for and retention of her driver's license transformed the encounter into an unlawful seizure, violating her Fourth Amendment rights.
defendant_argument: The defendant, State of Wisconsin, argued that the initial interaction was consensual, and VanBeek was not seized until asked to exit her truck, or alternatively, that the officer had reasonable suspicion for any seizure.
decision_outcome: The court reversed the circuit court's judgment of conviction and remanded with instructions to grant VanBeek's motion to suppress, concluding the seizure was unlawful due to a lack of reasonable suspicion.

title: State v. Halverson
description: Whether an incarcerated individual is per se in custody for purposes of Miranda warnings, or if a 'totality of the circumstances' test applies, and whether Brian Halverson was in custody under that test.
plaintiff_argument: The plaintiff, State of Wisconsin, argued that federal law (Howes v. Fields) rejects a per se rule for Miranda custody for incarcerated individuals, and that under a totality of the circumstances test, Halverson was not in custody due to the phone interview's nature and brevity.
defendant_argument: The defendant, Brian L. Halverson, argued that all incarcerated individuals should be considered in custody for Miranda purposes due to their incarceration, or alternatively, that the totality of circumstances in his case still demonstrated he was in custody.
decision_outcome: The court affirmed the court of appeals' decision, ruling that Halverson was not in custody for Miranda purposes and, therefore, his motion to suppress statements should have been denied.
